# My Journal Entry

Today, young Tanaka from the next village came to help me set up a smartphone. At first, I was nervous—all those tiny buttons and confusing screens! But he was patient, showing me how to use the camera to photograph my vegetable garden, and how to send messages to my daughter in Tokyo. I must admit, seeing her face on the screen when we video-called this evening... it brought tears to my eyes. Technology isn't so frightening when it serves what matters most.

This morning, I had an idea. What if we used these devices to help preserve our village's stories? I mentioned it to the community center director, and she became excited. Perhaps we could record the old songs and recipes that only the elders know, before they're forgotten. The younger people might actually listen if it's on their phones! This feels different from the mindless modernization I feared. This is technology with purpose—it protects our traditions rather than replaces them.

Still, I worry sometimes. I see young people staring at screens instead of looking at the mountains or talking to their neighbors. But I'm learning that technology itself isn't the enemy; it's how we choose to use it.
